agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Even though the majority of the pancreatic islet cell cancers are indolent, an aggressive approach is preferred to the “wait-and-see” approach when dealing with them. The aggressive approach includes taking out the primary and metastatic tumors. However, the implementation of the aggressive approach requires a team comprised of multiple specialties to deal with pre and post-surgical management. [20] The aggressive therapy for PNETs involves surgery, locoregional therapy followed by systemic therapy, and complication control. [21]

agent1_snippets.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Technique or Treatment
agent1_snippets.content: Distal Pancreatectomy with islet cell autotransplantation - Over the last 10 to 15 years, this technique has become much more commonly performed after total pancreatectomy for chronic pancreatitis. Total pancreatectomy removes all endocrine and exocrine function from the patient. Autotransplantation allows the patient to have an endocrine function even after the removal of the pancreas. Patient selection and decisions on who will really benefit from autotransplantation are key factors that must be discussed. Patients must undergo testing to validate adequate islet cell number and function. There are multiple contraindications to performing total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation: if the patient has an active substance abuse, pancreatic cancer, or poorly controlled psychiatric illnesses. If the patient is determined to be a candidate for total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation, the transplantation can occur during the same operative intervention. After the pancreas is removed, it is placed on the back table for preparation. The pancreas is placed into ice-cold water with antibiotic solution and the preservation solution. The gastroduodenal and splenic arteries are then injected with a preservation solution. The pancreatic duct can then be cannulated on the table with an injection of a preservation solution with a mixture of different enzymes. The gland is injected until fully distributed throughout the parenchyma. The pancreas can then be cut into small 5 mm sections. The sections can then be placed into a Ricordi digestion chamber. The chamber can then be shaken. The chamber solution can then undergo microscopy for adequate islet cell count and application of human albumin with centrifugal treatment. The specimen can then be prepared for injection into the portal vein system. To avoid any portal vein thrombosis or portal hypertensive complications, the specimen can be injected with heparin running intravenously. The goal of this treatment is to have the patient remain insulin-free. Studies have shown that up to one-third of patients remain insulin-free while another one third will have a partial function of their islet cell transplantation. The overall goal of total pancreatectomy with islet autotransplantation is to relieve the patient of chronic pain and to reserve their endocrine function to avoid chronic insulin use. [19] [20]

agent1_snippets.title: Distal Pancreatectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Arterial Supply of the Pancreas
agent1_snippets.content: The vascular supply of the pancreas is very complex and is derived from both the celiac artery and the superior mesenteric artery. The common hepatic artery and splenic artery both arise from the celiac trunk. The common hepatic artery gives rise to the gastroduodenal artery (GDA); the GDA then gives rise to the right gastroepiploic artery after which it continues inferiorly becoming the anterior and posterior superior pancreaticoduodenal arteries. The superior mesenteric artery supplies both the inferior anterior and posterior pancreaticoduodenal arteries. These two superior and inferior pancreaticoduodenal arteries supply the pancreatic head, 2nd, and 3rd portions of the duodenum. The rich blood supply to this area is what allows duodenal sparing pancreatic surgery to be performed. The remaining portions of the pancreas are supplied by the splenic artery and superior mesenteric artery. The splenic artery runs along the superior aspect of the pancreas and gives off many branches. Three named arteries arise from the splenic artery, which runs perpendicular across the pancreatic body and tail. These arteries are named from medial to lateral- the dorsal, great, and caudal pancreatic arteries. These three arteries help form collateralization between the splenic artery and the inferior pancreaticoduodenal arteries. Overall, the pancreas has a rich blood supply, which must be taken into account when planning any surgical procedure involving this gland. High-quality CT imaging is a must, not only to view the pathological disease process (i.e., cancer or lesion) but to have an excellent visualization of the vessels during surgical planning. This could make the cancerous lesion unresectable. While there can be some disagreement regarding the exact definition of a borderline resectable lesion, in general, these all involve vascular determination, including- abutment of the superior mesenteric artery, celiac artery, or hepatic artery, all less than 180 degrees or have very small areas that are occluded along the portal vein, the superior mesenteric vein that may possibly have the need for reconstruction. Tumors that circumferentially encase the celiac artery, hepatic artery, or the superior mesenteric artery greater than 180 degrees or have complete occlusion of the superior mesenteric vein or portal vein with no possibility of reconstruction are considered locally advanced and surgically unresectable. [2] CT imaging also provides the surgeon with the chance to review any anatomical variants that may be present. A replaced right hepatic artery is the most common arterial variant, with the artery arises from the superior mesenteric artery. This variant is found in roughly 15% of the population. The replaced artery runs posterior and superior to the superior mesenteric artery and then posterior to the portal vein along the right side of the porta hepatis. Accidental injury to this artery can result in liver ischemia or cause a newly formed anastomosis to fail. Another anatomical variant is a replaced left hepatic artery, which occurs in less than 10% of the population. The replaced artery arises from the left gastric artery, travels along the superior aspect of the lesser omentum, and can be involved in pancreatic body tumors on rare occasions.

agent1_snippets.title: Pancreatic Cancer -- Surgical Oncology
agent1_snippets.content: The neoadjuvant first approach in resectable pancreatic adenocarcinoma is implemented more and more frequently at high-volume centers nationwide and internationally. The rationale behind the neoadjuvant first approach is that the patient is in the best shape to receive chemotherapy and at the best odds of completing chemotherapy for 4 to 6 months. In addition, tissue is thought to be well-oxygenated, having not undergone a large procedure such as the Whipple. Many patients do not complete or even start adjuvant chemotherapy after surgical resection, thereby decreasing their survival. [13]

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1905
agent2_snippets.content: Benign tumor cells are not regarded as a finding of pancreatic cancer (السرطان البنكرياسي), indicating that their presence does not support a diagnosis of this malignancy. In contrast, tumor cells whose nature is uncertain—whether benign or malignant—are considered abnormal cells of pancreatic cancer, reflecting their potential relevance to the disease process. Recognizing the difference between clearly benign cells and cells of indeterminate behavior is essential for accurate pathological assessment and appropriate patient management in the context of pancreatic cancer.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1267
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes several specific pancreatic endocrine disorders. Among its children are ارتفاع السكر في الدم, which describes the elevated blood‑glucose levels that characterize hyperglycemia; مرض السكري دون مضاعفات, which refers to diabetes that has not yet produced secondary complications; فرط أنسولين الدم, indicating an excess of circulating insulin often seen in early insulin‑resistance states or insulin‑producing tumors; and ورم غلوكاغوني, a rare glucagon‑secreting tumor (glucagonoma) that can also cause hyperglycemia. These conditions are interrelated through their impact on pancreatic hormone production and regulation of blood glucose, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and management of pancreatic endocrine function in patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2386
agent2_snippets.content: Malignant Cellular Infiltrate is recognized as a pathological finding in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي (pancreatic cancer), indicating that this infiltrative pattern can be present across different organ‑specific malignancies. Likewise, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as abnormal cells of disease in both ovarian cancer and pancreatic cancer, underscoring their role as a hallmark of malignant transformation regardless of tissue origin. In addition, Tumor cells, uncertain whether benign or malignant are also classified as abnormal cells of disease in both cancer of ovary and السرطان البنكرياسي, reflecting the diagnostic challenge posed by indeterminate cellular morphology. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2721
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that includes ارتفاع السكر في الدم, اعتلال عصبي سكري, فرط أنسولين الدم, and ورم غلوكاغوني. ارتفاع السكر في الدم (high blood sugar) is a primary manifestation of diabetes, reflecting impaired glucose regulation by the endocrine pancreas. اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy) is a common chronic complication of prolonged hyperglycemia, affecting peripheral nerves and leading to sensory and motor deficits. فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia) denotes excessive insulin secretion, which can occur in early insulin resistance or insulin‑producing tumors and may precede or accompany diabetes. ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma) is a rare pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes excess glucagon, often resulting in persistent hyperglycemia and weight loss. Recognizing how these conditions are interrelated within endocrine pancreatic disease supports comprehensive diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic strategies for affected patients.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
agent2_snippets.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is a broad category that encompasses several specific conditions. Among its subentities are اعتلال عصبي سكري (diabetic neuropathy), a chronic complication in which prolonged hyperglycemia leads to damage of peripheral nerves and results in sensory loss, pain, or autonomic dysfunction. The category also includes فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), a state of elevated insulin levels in the bloodstream that can arise from insulin resistance, early type 2 diabetes, or insulin‑secreting tumors and contributes to metabolic disturbances such as hypoglycemia and weight gain. Another child condition is ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma), a rare neoplasm of the pancreatic α‑cells that secretes excess glucagon, producing a characteristic syndrome of hyperglycemia, necrolytic migratory erythema, weight loss, and anemia. Finally, the category lists ضمور نصف الوجه المترقي (progressive hemifacial atrophy), an uncommon, often autoimmune‑mediated disorder characterized by gradual wasting of the skin, subcutaneous tissue, and muscle on one side of the face; its inclusion reflects the recognition that endocrine pancreatic diseases, particularly those with autoimmune components, may be associated with extra‑pancreatic manifestations. Together, these entities illustrate the diverse clinical spectrum—from metabolic and neuropathic complications to neoplastic and autoimmune manifestations—linked to diseases of the endocrine pancreas, underscoring the importance of comprehensive evaluation and multidisciplinary management in affected patients.
